skill.name: when-managing-token-budget-use-token-budget-advisor
skill.description: Plans large AI tasks against a token budget by estimating complexity, defining chunks, and adding checkpoints.
skill.summary: Create practical token budget plans for complex AI work.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: The static weak-cryptography and Ruby backtick findings are false positives from markdown examples, Graphviz labels, and ordinary planning text. The skill does document `npx claude-flow@alpha` commands, so publication should include a warning that users approve external command execution.

medium_findings.title: External Command Examples Require User Approval
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends `npx claude-flow@alpha` hook and memory commands. This supports the stated workflow, but `npx` can download and execute npm package code, so commands should only run after explicit user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and repeated. The context is legitimate documentation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines are Graphviz settings, markdown examples, and task-planning text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner pattern does not match real cryptographic code in the reviewed context. Confidence is low because this is a dismissed static finding, not a confirmed vulnerability.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections come from fenced markdown code blocks. The files are documentation and do not contain executable Ruby backtick expressions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show markdown fences and example commands, not Ruby interpolation or executable source. This is very likely scanner noise.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented `npx` Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell examples that call `npx claude-flow@alpha`. This is not malicious by itself, but it is a dangerous pattern if an agent runs the commands without user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present and could execute external package code. The surrounding text frames them as workflow examples, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

content.user_title: Plan Token Budgets for AI Tasks
content.value_statement: Large AI tasks can exceed context limits before important work is complete. This skill estimates effort, divides work into chunks, and adds checkpoints for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Assesses current usage against a token limit and safety buffer.
Estimates task complexity from task type, agents, integrations, tests, and documentation.
Creates chunking strategies with dependencies, priorities, and expected outputs.
Builds execution plans with checkpoints and budget tracking tables.
Defines contingency actions when actual usage exceeds the estimate.
limitations: Token estimates are heuristic and need user review.
It cannot read live token usage unless the host tool provides that data.
Command examples depend on external claude-flow tooling.
It does not enforce budget limits automatically.

use_cases.title: Plan a Large Feature
use_cases.description: Estimate the token cost of a feature and split work into ordered chunks before coding starts.

use_cases.title: Recover From Context Pressure
use_cases.description: Create a smaller execution plan when a session is near its token limit.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Work
use_cases.description: Assign phases, dependencies, checkpoints, and outputs across planning and code analysis agents.

prompt_templates.title: Estimate a Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the token budget advisor for this task: [task]. My token limit is [limit], and I have used [used].

prompt_templates.title: Create a Chunk Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Split a large task into safe phases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a chunking plan for [task]. Include estimates, dependencies, priorities, outputs, and checkpoint points.

prompt_templates.title: Adjust for Low Budget
prompt_templates.scenario: Continue work near the context limit.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have [remaining] tokens left and need to finish [goal]. Prioritize the remaining work and defer noncritical items.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Full Execution Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a token budget execution plan for [project]. Include complexity factors, chunk sequence, tracking table, contingency actions, and success criteria.

output_examples.input: Plan a full-stack authentication system with 155,000 tokens remaining.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a healthy budget status, a high-confidence estimate, four implementation chunks, checkpoint steps, and a safety margin.

output_examples.input: I have 20,000 tokens left and still need tests and documentation.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends critical tests first, defers low-value documentation, and adds a checkpoint after each compact task.

output_examples.input: Coordinate planning, implementation, testing, and review across agents.
output_examples.output: The skill creates phase boundaries, names required outputs, lists dependencies, and tracks estimated token use per phase.

best_practices: Provide current usage, total limit, and the task goal before asking for a plan.
Keep a safety buffer for review, testing, and handoff work.
Update estimates after each checkpoint with actual usage.
anti_patterns: Using the estimate as an exact guarantee.
Running documented shell commands without reviewing them first.
Starting a large task without defining outputs for each chunk.

faq.question: Does this skill reduce token usage automatically?
faq.answer: No. It creates plans and estimates that help the user manage token usage.

faq.question: Can it use live token counts?
faq.answer: Only when the host environment provides usage data or the user supplies current counts.

faq.question: Why does it use a safety buffer?
faq.answer: The buffer reserves space for unexpected debugging, review, and final response work.

faq.question: Is it useful for small tasks?
faq.answer: It is most useful for complex tasks, long sessions, or work near a token limit.

faq.question: Does it require claude-flow?
faq.answer: No. The planning guidance is usable without it, but some examples reference claude-flow commands.

faq.question: How should teams use the chunk plan?
faq.answer: Teams should review estimates, confirm dependencies, and checkpoint actual usage after each chunk.
